era_context: The Late Bronze Age collapse is in its final stages or recently concluded (c. 1200–1050 BCE). The Eastern Mediterranean and Near East are experiencing profound upheaval: the Mycenaean palatial system has fallen, the Hittite Empire has dissolved, and Egypt is in the Third Intermediate Period under weak pharaohs. The Iron Age is beginning in the Levant and Greece. In China, the Shang dynasty is in its final century before the Zhou conquest. In India, the Vedic period is well established. Stability is fragmentary; regional powers are reasserting themselves.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited archaeological record for this period)
Northern Europe (sparse written sources; archaeological record exists but underrepresented in classical historiography)
Mesoamerica (no written records; archaeological dating uncertain for this specific year)

events.title: Reign of Ramesses III in Egypt (traditional dating)
events.description: Ramesses III, the last great pharaoh of the New Kingdom, is in the latter part of his reign (c. 1186–1155 BCE by conventional chronology). He faced invasions by the Sea Peoples and Libyan incursions, which he repelled. His reign marks the end of Egyptian imperial power in the Levant and the beginning of the Third Intermediate Period's fragmentation. By 1045 BCE, his authority is waning, and the priesthood of Amun at Thebes is accumulating unprecedented wealth and autonomy.
events.certainty_note: Ramesses III's reign dates are well-established from inscriptions and papyri, though absolute chronology of the 20th Dynasty remains debated (±10–15 years possible). The year 1045 BCE falls within his documented reign by conventional dating.

events.title: Late Shang dynasty in China; Zhou conquest imminent
events.description: The Shang dynasty is in its final decades. King Di Xin (帝辛, also known as Zhou of Shang) rules from Yin. The Zhou people, a vassal state in the Wei River valley, are consolidating power under King Wen and his son King Wu. By 1045 BCE, the Shang state is weakened by internal strife, military overextension, and loss of tributary support. The Zhou conquest will occur within the next few years (traditionally dated to 1046 or 1045 BCE, though modern scholarship suggests c. 1050–1040 BCE).
key_figures: Di Xin / Zhou of Shang (Shang king)
King Wu of Zhou (Zhou leader, not yet ruling)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian
Archaeological evidence from Yinxu and Zhou sites

events.certainty_note: The Shang-Zhou transition is dated variously from 1122 BCE (traditional) to 1040 BCE (modern archaeology). The year 1045 BCE is within the probable window. Oracle bones and bronze inscriptions confirm the late Shang context, but precise annual dating is not possible.

events.title: Iron Age settlement expansion in the Levant
events.description: In the aftermath of the Late Bronze Age collapse, Iron Age I settlements are expanding across the Levant. Philistine settlements are established in the coastal plain (Gaza, Ashkelon, Ashdod, Gath, Ekron). Aramean kingdoms are consolidating in inland Syria. Hebrew-speaking groups are settling in the hill country of Canaan. Iron tools and weapons are becoming more common, though bronze remains in use. This period sees the emergence of the political entities that will dominate the Iron Age Levant.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Iron Age I sites (Megiddo, Tel Dan, Ashkelon, Ekron)

events.certainty_note: Iron Age I in the Levant is well-attested archaeologically and dated to c. 1200–1000 BCE. The year 1045 BCE falls within this period. Specific events for this year are not documented; this entry describes the broader context.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional dating of the Shang-Zhou conquest to 1046 BCE (or 1045 BCE) is increasingly questioned by modern scholars. Radiocarbon dating and archaeological evidence suggest the conquest occurred closer to 1050–1040 BCE, possibly even later. The Sima Qian chronology, which places it at 1046 BCE, is now viewed as schematic rather than historically precise. Additionally, the 'Late Bronze Age collapse' narrative, while dominant, is contested: some scholars argue for a more gradual transition rather than a catastrophic collapse, and the role of the Sea Peoples is debated.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1045 BCE is heavily skewed toward Egypt and China, where written records and inscriptions survive. The Levant is known primarily through archaeology, which cannot pinpoint events to a single year. Sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, and the Americas are essentially undocumented for this year. The Egyptian record is court-sponsored and may exaggerate military victories. The Chinese record is filtered through much later compilations (Sima Qian, 1st century BCE) and may contain legendary elements. Absolute chronology for this period remains contested, with ±10–20 year uncertainties common.

graph_edges.note: Continuation of late Shang decline and Zhou consolidation
Iron Age I settlement expansion in the Levant (c. 1200–1000 BCE)
